responses.content: WAL mode lets readers proceed concurrently with a single writer because commits append to the write-ahead log instead of overwriting database pages in place, so readers keep a consistent snapshot. Trade-offs versus a rollback journal: faster writes and non-blocking readers, at the cost of an extra -wal file, periodic checkpointing, and slightly slower reads on very large unchecked WALs.

This looks like shared-state flakiness across tests; escalating for deeper concurrency analysis.

arguments.reason: cross-test race condition needs deeper analysis

responses.content: The race: both tests mutate the same module-level cache, so ordering changes the outcome. Isolate the fixture with tmp_path and reset the cache in teardown; the test then passes in any order.

Total distance is 120 km; time is 2 h out + 1 h back = 3 h, so the average speed is 40 km/h. The common 45 km/h answer is the arithmetic-mean trap — average speed must be total distance over total time.

issues: 45 km/h is the arithmetic mean of the two speeds; average speed must use total distance over total time
verdicts.next_action: Recompute using total distance divided by total time

WebGPU now ships enabled by default in Chrome and Edge; Firefox ships it on Windows with other platforms in flight, and Safari previews it behind a feature flag — so cross-browser support is close but not yet universal.

At 1000 pages (~4 MB WAL) the autocheckpoint threshold is reasonable, but a write-heavy workload risks checkpoint starvation while long-lived readers pin the WAL: the file keeps growing and read latency creeps up. Monitor -wal file size and run PRAGMA wal_checkpoint(TRUNCATE) during idle windows.
